title: The Gospel as the Keystone of Righteous Living
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander

outline.title: Introduction: The Explanatory 'For' of Verse 11
subpoints: Titus 2:11 begins with 'for' — an explanatory conjunction that connects the entire list of conduct commands in Titus 1-2 (older men, older women, young women, young men, bondservants) back to a single reason
The reason for all right living is the gospel; right living is not the engine but the exhaust of the gospel
Grace as keystone: a keystone is the central stone of an arch that holds all the other stones in place; remove it and everything falls; the gospel is the keystone of righteous Christian living

outline.title: 1. Grace Has Appeared to All People
subpoints: GRACE: God's Riches At Christ's Expense — an acronym for the definition of grace
'To all people' (v.11) does not teach universal salvation; in context, it refers to all types of people — matching the groups listed in vv.2-10 (old men, old women, young women, young men, slaves)
The gospel is not limited to one social class, ethnic group, or gender; it has appeared for all kinds of people

outline.title: 2. Grace Trains
subpoints: Verse 12: grace 'trains us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions' — the word 'trains' (paideuousa) is the word for the training of a child, including instruction and discipline
Grace does not produce passive Christians; it produces people who actively deny what is contrary to God
The two-part shape of sanctification: put off (deny ungodliness and worldly lusts) and put on (live soberly, righteously, and godly in the present age)
Sanctification is not something that happens to us automatically but something grace trains us to pursue

outline.title: 3. The Blessed Hope
subpoints: Verse 13: 'waiting for our blessed hope, the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ' — this is the only place in Scripture that explicitly calls the second coming 'the blessed hope'
A 'hope' in Scripture is not a wish or an aspiration but a certainty not yet realized; it is the evidence of things not seen
The world offers many 'hopes' — retirement, health, financial security — but these are contingent and uncertain; the blessed hope is certain
The second coming is not a threat for the believer but a promise — the appearing of the one who gave himself for us

outline.title: 4. Christ's Work: Voluntary, Exhaustive, Substitutionary
subpoints: Verse 14: 'who gave himself for us' — voluntary; Christ was not a victim but a willing substitute
'To redeem us from all lawlessness' — exhaustive; he did not purchase partial redemption but complete deliverance from the penalty and power of all sin

'And to purify for himself a people for his own possession who are zealous for good works' — substitutionary and purposeful; the goal of redemption is a people who reflect the character of their redeemer
Zealous for good works: the gift of the Spirit produces good works through varied, God-given gifting (Romans 12:3-8); zeal does not look the same in every person

summary: A Sunday school lesson by Elder Scott Hollander on Titus 2:11-15, tracing how the gospel is the explanatory foundation — the keystone — of all the conduct commands in Titus 1-2. The 'for' of verse 11 connects every exhortation to older men, older women, young women, young men, and slaves back to the appearing of grace. Four movements: (1) grace has appeared — to all types of people (not universal salvation, but all groups of Titus 2); (2) grace trains — actively, as a child is trained — to deny ungodliness and live soberly, righteously, and godly; (3) the blessed hope — the only explicit use of this phrase in Scripture for the second coming; a biblical hope is a certainty, not a wish; (4) Christ's work as voluntary, exhaustive, and substitutionary — redeeming from all lawlessness and producing a people zealous for good works, with diverse Spirit-given gifting (Romans 12:3-8).
transcript:
## Introduction: The Explanatory 'For'

We're looking at Titus 2:11-15 this morning. Before we get to those verses, let me remind you what Paul has been doing in chapters 1 and 2.

He has given Titus a series of conduct commands: older men are to be sober, reverent, temperate. Older women are to behave as holy women, not slanderers. Young women are to love their husbands, love their children, be discreet, chaste, homemakers. Young men are to be sober-minded. Bondservants are to be obedient. That is a lot of imperatives.

And then verse 11 begins with the word 'for.' For. That is an explanatory conjunction. It is answering the question: why? Why should older men be sober? Why should young women love their husbands? Why should bondservants be obedient? Because — *for* — the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men.

All right living flows from the gospel. The gospel is not an add-on to the Christian life. It is the foundation. It is the reason. You could picture it as a keystone — the central stone of an arch that holds every other stone in place. Remove the keystone and the arch collapses. The gospel holds everything else together.

## 1. Grace Has Appeared to All People

Verse 11: *For the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men.*

Grace is a word worth pausing on. You may have heard it defined as an acronym: God's Riches At Christ's Expense. That is not a bad place to start. Grace is God giving what we do not deserve, at a cost born entirely by Christ.

And it has appeared 'to all men' — or in some translations, 'to all people.' This does not teach that every human being will be saved. Look at the context: Paul has just addressed older men, older women, young women, young men, and bondservants — different groups of people. 'All people' here means all types of people. The grace of God is not restricted to one ethnic group, one social class, one gender, one era. It has appeared for all kinds of people.

## 2. Grace Trains

Verse 12: *...training us to deny ungodliness and worldly lusts, and to live soberly, righteously, and godly in the present age.*

The word 'training' here is the Greek *paideuousa* — the same word used for the education and discipline of a child. This is not passive. Grace is not just a warm feeling that washes over you and leaves you unchanged. Grace is active. It trains. It instructs. It disciplines.

And notice the two movements: first, deny — turn away from ungodliness and worldly lusts. Second, live — soberly, righteously, and godly. This is the shape of sanctification throughout the New Testament: put off and put on. Colossians 3: put off the old man, put on the new. Ephesians 4: put off lying, put on truth; put off bitterness, put on kindness.

Grace trains us to do this. Not our own willpower. Not our own resolution. The grace of God, working in us through the Spirit, produces the kind of life that reflects our redeemer.

## 3. The Blessed Hope

Verse 13: *...looking for the blessed hope and glorious appearing of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ.*

I want you to notice the phrase 'blessed hope.' This is the only place in the entire Bible that explicitly calls the second coming of Christ 'the blessed hope.' It is worth sitting with.

A biblical 'hope' is not the same as what we mean when we say 'I hope the weather is nice.' Biblical hope is a certainty not yet realized. It is the assurance of things not seen. We do not hope in the second coming the way we hope our team wins the championship. We know it is coming. The question is only when.

The world offers many hopes: a comfortable retirement, good health, financial security, successful children. These are conditional. They depend on circumstances you cannot fully control. The blessed hope is unconditional. It rests entirely on the character and the promise of God.

And for the believer, the second coming is not a threat — it is the appearing of the one who gave himself for us. We are looking forward to seeing him.

## 4. Christ's Work

Verse 14: *...who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from every lawless deed and purify for himself his own special people, zealous for good works.*

Three things about what Christ did.

First: he *gave himself*. This was voluntary. Jesus was not a victim of circumstances. He was not caught off guard by the cross. He gave himself — willingly, deliberately, knowingly.

Second: he redeemed us *from every lawless deed* — from all lawlessness. This is exhaustive. He did not purchase a partial redemption that covers most of your sin. He redeemed from all of it. The penalty of all your sin — past, present, and future — was laid on him.

Third: the purpose — *to purify for himself a people for his own possession, zealous for good works.* The goal of redemption is not merely forgiven people. It is a purified people. A people who belong to him. A people who are *zealous* for good works — not reluctantly compliant, but eager.

And this zeal looks different in different people. Romans 12:3-8 describes a variety of gifts — prophecy, serving, teaching, exhorting, giving, leading, mercy — each distributed differently, each expressing the same zeal. You don't have to be zealous in the same way as the person next to you. But the grace of God produces zeal in every one of its recipients.

Father, thank you for the keystone — for the grace that holds all of life together. Help us to live in light of it, trained by it, looking for the blessed hope it promises. In Jesus' name, amen.
